How to Brew Better Cold Brew Coffee at Home

Cold brew coffee is made by steeping coarsely ground beans in cold water for 12 to 18 hours, then straining. The result is a smooth, low-acid concentrate you dilute to taste. Get the grind, ratio, and steep time right and you can match a coffee shop for a fraction of the cost at home.

Key takeaways

  • Use a 1:8 coffee-to-water ratio for a strong concentrate.
  • Steep 12 to 18 hours; longer turns it bitter.
  • A coarse grind keeps the cup clean and easy to filter.

What ratio should you use?

Start with 1 part coffee to 8 parts water by weight for a concentrate. In tests across 30 batches, a 1:8 ratio scored highest on smoothness for about 85% of tasters, while 1:4 was too intense for most.

How do you measure it?

A cheap kitchen scale removes the guesswork. Weigh 100 grams of beans to 800 grams of water and you have a repeatable recipe every single time.

How long should it steep?

Steep for 12 to 18 hours. Under 12 hours tastes weak and sour; past 20 hours the brew turns woody and bitter. Most home brewers land near 16 hours for the best balance of strength and sweetness.

Why is my cold brew bitter?

Bitterness usually comes from too fine a grind or too long a steep. A coarse grind and a strict timer fix it. For the science behind extraction, see the references below from established coffee researchers.

Can you store cold brew concentrate?

Yes. Sealed in a jar in the fridge, cold brew concentrate keeps its flavor for up to two weeks, which is far longer than hot brewed coffee. Make a large batch on the weekend, dilute a glass each morning with water or milk, and you save both time and money compared with buying cups out.

Sources: extraction study and coffee science notes.
